title: F1R - Fugi Visualizer
role: AI MUSIC PROJECT / CODEX / ASCII VISUALIZER
description: F1R is my AI-based music and character art project. This page focuses on the Fugi lyric visualizer I built with Codex: a live browser transmission with an animated ASCII face, audio-reactive motion, synced lyric glitches, and F1R character explorations.
body: F1R is the band/project name for an AI-based music and visual identity experiment. `Fugi` is one track inside that world, and the lyric visualizer is only one output from a larger character-driven art direction: music generation, cyberpunk portrait experiments, browser animation, and a fictional hacked transmission language.
The live version is here: [open the Fugi visualizer](https://lilbadger.github.io/fugi-vizualizer/).

I used Codex as a build partner to turn the Suno track into a browser-native lyric video. The page is not a rendered video file: it draws the performance live with HTML Canvas, a source portrait, timed lyric cues, procedural glitch cards, and audio-driven motion.

The important part is the workflow. I could describe an idea in motion-design language, inspect what the browser was doing, push the code toward a stronger image, and keep iterating until the piece felt less like a tech demo and more like a tiny F1R transmission.

The visualizer converts this portrait into an ASCII signal. The browser crops the head, rejects most of the flat blue background, samples color and brightness into character cells, then gives the face a fake depth model so it can yaw, push, trail, and tear with the song.

The signal chain is intentionally direct: song plus portrait go in; the browser generates the ASCII face, motion, trails, scanlines, HUD noise, and lyric bursts in real time. Codex helped iterate the canvas architecture, timing data, and visual system quickly enough that the tool started feeling more like a live instrument than a static page.

The portrait is cropped into a face-focused signal, sampled into a character grid, rebuilt as a colored ASCII texture, then pushed through a fake depth model. Audio bands drive the face yaw, pitch, push, trails, and glitch density so the head feels like it is reacting to the track instead of just looping.

This is the kind of AI art project I want to keep expanding: generated music, generated identity, code, timing, motion design, and web-native performance all meeting inside one object. The Fugi lyric video is a small part of F1R, but it proves the pipeline can make a song feel like a character, an interface, and a world.
This glitch portrait sits closest to the final visualizer language: neon rabbit mask, blue hacked interface, anonymous user readouts, and a noisy signal texture around the face.

The wider cinematic look explores how the F1R character could exist in a real city environment: wet pavement, Bucharest-night energy, long-exposure light trails, and the rabbit mask treated as a diegetic neon device.

This version pushes the identity into a stranger direction, turning the face into architecture. It is not just a portrait variation; it suggests that F1R could be a person, a place, a broadcast, or an infected city surface.

After the browser visualizer was working, I started testing how the F1R language could move outside the ASCII interface. One direction was Seedance 2.0: I was experimenting with ways to generate shots that feel longer than the usual short AI-video fragment, trying to keep the motion connected enough that it can read as a bigger sequence instead of a disconnected clip.

I also tested LTX locally, looking for the best quality I could get from a local video-generation setup. The differences can be subtle; after a point, the choice becomes less about one tool being magically better and more about budget, render time, hardware, control, and how much cleanup a shot can survive.

These stills explore the F1R world as higher-resolution illustrations rather than realtime browser graphics. The process mixed ChatGPT image work with local image models for cleanup and upscaling, then Photoshop color adjustments to pull the final frames closer to the same neon, wet, hacked-signal mood.

title: Night of the Living Dead - LTX-2 Contest
description: For an LTX-2 contest, I rebuilt a complete sequence from Night of the Living Dead as generated footage while keeping the supplied soundtrack and editorial duration fixed so the result could drop back into the film.
body: The contest started with one sequence from the 1968 film `Night of the Living Dead`. I could redesign every image, but the supplied audio and the 108.885-second editorial duration had to remain the spine of the piece. The generated shots therefore had to land on the existing performances, cuts, pauses, and sound cues closely enough for the replacement sequence to fit back into the full movie.

I split the scene into a shot-by-shot package and carried the original timing through the whole workflow. The archive eventually grew into 22 working shot folders, each holding experiments, structural passes, restyles, composites, motion generations, and selects. That organization mattered because this was not one hero shot; it was a continuous edit that had to survive from first frame to last.

Before asking an image model for a new world, I extracted controls from the source. HED edges held onto silhouettes and important object boundaries, while depth passes gave the restyle and motion stages a stronger understanding of foreground, background, and spatial separation.
![HED edge guide extracted from the source shot](assets/projects/night-of-the-living-dead-ltx-contest/structure-hed.png)
![Depth guide used to preserve the shot layout](assets/projects/night-of-the-living-dead-ltx-contest/structure-depth.png)
These guides were not treated as finished images. They were constraints: enough structure to preserve the shot, but enough freedom for the generated sequence to become a different visual world.

I used Qwen Image Edit inside ComfyUI for the main restyle stage. Multi-reference conditioning let me push a consistent graphic direction across different source shots, while prompt iterations and a Lightning LoRA made it practical to test variations quickly. I was looking for a sickly green and cyan broadcast-horror language built around CRT screens, hard comic shading, and exaggerated characters.

The restyle alone was rarely enough. I combined and upscaled selected elements so faces, screens, props, and silhouettes were stable before motion generation. That gave LTX-2 a cleaner starting image and reduced the amount of repair needed after each pass.

For motion I worked with LTX-2 19B through several controlled paths rather than relying on a single image-to-video prompt. Pose, canny, depth, image conditioning, and audio conditioning could be mixed according to what a shot needed. Short tests helped me diagnose motion before committing to longer generations.

DWPose was especially useful when the source performance had readable body language that needed to survive the redesign. For crowded shots I assembled a more stable multi-character image first, then used that composite as the motion source.

The original soundtrack was more than a delivery requirement: it also became a conditioning signal. Audio-guided LTX-2 passes helped movement and performance energy respond to the existing dialogue and rhythm while I kept the source edit locked.

The multi-character shots needed more iteration because consistency, eyelines, action, and screen direction all had to work at once. This selected WIP shows the point where the generated motion began to behave like part of a sequence rather than an isolated AI clip.

This is the compact LTXV setup I used for one of the selected shots. Two prepared image states are preprocessed and supplied as first/last-frame controls, while a trimmed section of the source audio drives the generation. The central settings group keeps the LTX-2 checkpoint, distilled/detail/camera-control LoRAs, prompt, 1432 x 1088 output size, 153-frame length, 24 fps, seed, CFG, and steps visible in one place before Video Combine writes the result.

I also tested camera reinterpretations instead of tracing every source frame literally. Close, wide, side, aerial, and low-angle prompts let me look for a more authored shot while still respecting the editorial function of the original beat.

The final stage was selection and repair: choose the strongest generations, reject motion that broke continuity, check cut points against the fixed soundtrack, and assemble the sequence at 24 fps in Blender. I preserved the canonical final export's audio stream in the web version above.
![Final sequence frame - CRT carried through the doorway](assets/projects/night-of-the-living-dead-ltx-contest/final-frame-doorway.jpg)

Long-form generated video is mainly a continuity problem. A beautiful single shot is easy to admire and easy to replace; a sequence has to preserve timing, character identity, eyelines, screen direction, action, and the intention of every cut. The strongest workflow was not one model or one prompt. It was a controlled loop between source analysis, restyle, motion tests, repair, and editorial judgment.

title: Cat Walkman
description: AI illustration experiment built with SDXL and ControlNet workflows, with music created in Suno.
body: I made this image in Invoke while experimenting with SDXL models and ControlNets. The main idea was to keep the generated image under direction instead of just letting the model improvise: block the composition, steer the shapes with control inputs, iterate the prompt and model response, then keep pushing the frame until the illustration felt like a finished cover image rather than a raw generation.

The making-of video shows that the image is built through iteration: composition first, then detail passes, cleanup, and final mood. I also made the music in Suno, so the project became a small AI illustration and music experiment rather than only a still image.

title: Daft Punk cover art
description: AI-driven cover-art workflow using ComfyUI, SDXL, posing tools, and Photoshop cleanup for multi-character direction.

videos.title: Making of Daft Punk ComfyUI Illustration

body: I used ComfyUI and SDXL to build a controlled cover-art workflow with multiple elements and characters. The main challenge was not just generating one nice frame; it was directing the scene: posing the characters, keeping the two figures readable, mixing the car/environment/helmet shapes together, and then upscaling the result without losing the cover-art composition.

For the character pose pass I used Reallusion Character Creator to create OpenPose ControlNet skeletons. Photoshop came in for small positional edits and cleanup early in the process, while ComfyUI handled the heavier image generation, mixing, refinement, and upscale steps. The result is an AI cover-art workflow where I still keep authorial control over staging and direction.

title: trips
description: AI environment experiment where I pushed current models and services to see how much quality I could get from strange alien-world drone footage.
body: I was playing with AI workflows to see how much quality I could squeeze out of the models and services available at the time. The goal was to create drone-like footage from a strange alien planet: unfamiliar, but still close enough to Earth that the environments feel physically readable.
The still images were generated in ComfyUI with Flux and upscaled there as well. I used those stills as starting frames in Runway, made the music with Aiva, and edited the final video in Blender.

title: Xparticles Challenge 2018 Animation Tests and Explorations
description: Animation tests and iterations made while exploring visual directions for the X-Particles Challenge 2018.
body: These clips collect the different passes I went through for the X-Particles Challenge 2018. They work best as a sequence: each short test is a slightly different look, camera direction, or procedural behavior while I was searching for the right motion language.
